does: CoSineVerifier presents a tool-augmented verification system designed to validate answers for computation-oriented scientific questions by integrating external computational executors and symbolic simplification tools. The core contribution is a two-stage pipeline that begins with cold-start fine-tuning on labeled verification examples, followed by multi-turn reinforcement learning with integrated tool use. Rather than relying on semantic matching alone, the verifier leverages external tools to perform precise algebraic equivalence checking, physical constant substitution, and symbolic simplifications. The method achieves state-of-the-art results on VerifyBench-Hard and SCI-Bench benchmarks, demonstrating strong performance across STEM subjects, general question-answering tasks, and long-form reasoning evaluation.
connects: This work extends the line of research on answer verification methods that have become critical infrastructure in modern language model training pipelines. It builds on prior unified verifier approaches while directly addressing their limitations in computation-intensive domains where semantic equivalence checkers fail. The paper connects to broader efforts in verifiable rewards for reinforcement learning and data curation, which increasingly require robust verification beyond simple string matching. It engages with symbolic computation and reasoning literature, particularly work on algebraic equivalence that typically employs computer algebra systems. The research also relates to long-standing challenges in evaluating scientific reasoning and STEM question-answering systems, where numerical and symbolic correctness matter fundamentally.
enables: CoSineVerifier opens pathways for more sophisticated training pipelines in scientific AI, enabling reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards on truly computation-dependent domains where verification accuracy directly impacts learning signal quality. The tool-augmented verification framework can be extended to other specialized domains requiring external computation, from formal mathematics to physics simulations. The two-stage training approach combining cold-start fine-tuning with tool-integrated RL provides a template for integrating external tools into verifier training more broadly. Future work can expand the external tool ecosystem, improve tool-selection mechanisms, and develop verifiers for increasingly complex scientific reasoning chains. This foundation enables building more trustworthy scientific AI systems where verification provides genuine correctness guarantees rather than probabilistic matching.
